sentences.text: I would say that there was an infirmary and people were really dying from starvation, and as well of typhus.
There were two doctors that I do recall in the whole town, so obviously that was quite a strain upon the doctors.
And I would say that there were not enough medication to heal.
They did create a ghetto whereby the people could not move out from the town.
It was very difficult for Zarki to create a ghetto to resettle, being that the town was a Jewish town.
There was no walls, no gates.
It was just on the outskirts of the city.
On all the roads there was police stations checking people going in and out of the city.
Absolutely.
They had to have an ID or permission to move out or go out of the city.
My father was a very comfortable man, as I stated before.
And at the same time I was speaking about my father, I'd like to bring back a memory that came to my mind at the present.
In 1939, when the first months of the German occupation, somehow, someway the Germans were scared for-- for an uprising, due to the forthcoming day of-- Independence Day of Poland.
The Independence Day of Poland was November 11.
Somehow, they were afraid.
The occupation was just the month of September, October, and here they're approaching November 11.
They came out with an ordinance, and they took four hostages of the city.
The four people that they took was a priest, a rabbi, a Gentile, a Jew.
One Jew.
And that one Jew was my father.

At that date, they locked them in and they said, if the town will not behave, or there will be any discomfort for the Germans or any uprising or any shootings or anything which-- extraordinary, the four will be killed.
It was quiet.
I don't know because of the four people, just people did not resist.
My father was released.
They kept them for 48 hours.
Going back to the ghetto, which people could not go out, obviously, a lot of people starting to go out without permission, without any means of papers.
If they were caught, there were killed.
And there was a lot of their killings.
I would say most of the guards were Germans with also some black police-- which, there were black uniforms, but there were Poles.
I did not-- I was too young to work.
And my father did not have to go to work.
They were taking people to various stations to work.
The stations, mainly, what they did was just to keep the highway clean.
The highway that ran through our city was one of the most important crossing points into the heart of Poland, and at the same time going west into the heart of Germany.

So that highway-- so to say, in those days, a highway-- it was not a six-lane highway like in Los Angeles or California-- but it was a highway, well-kept road, which they always tried to keep it clean, especially in the wintertime.
When snow fell, that road had to be constantly cleaned.
So, actually, what is the date of the start of the ghetto?
When did they actually start the ghetto?
I would say the ghetto-- towards the end-- 1940, 1941-- and was going on until 1942 during the high holiday period.
That's correct.
In 1942, there were liquidations of the ghetto.
We did say that it's really getting to be very, very, re-settling.
I do not know if the people really comprehend-- and especially myself as a child-- definitely could not comprehend that liquidation of a ghetto means that they sending us to the gas chamber.
When this came, most of the time, those-- those liquidation of ghettos were done in a sporadic and surprising attack, so to say.
They came-- mostly they picked a Jewish holiday.

May it be the Jewish New Year, the most sacred day of Yom Kippur, which is the Day of Atonement, or the other tabernacle holiday, Simchat Torah, the day we're were rejoicing with the Torah-- that's the day that they pick for liquidation, where there really was a surprise that you could not expect anything because it is a holiday.
Our city was destined to be liquidated on Simchat Torah.
We did not know-- of course, now we know that it was liquidated on Simchat Torah.
We run away from that city, just the day-- eight days before.
In other words, the first-- a day before the holidays we run away from that city.
We got-- we got the information, so to say.
We got some rumors that other towns were liquidated.
There was not telephone communications or any other means, but we just got the rumors that this town was liquidated, this town was liquidated.
So most likely that the town of Zarki probably will be liquidated within the next days or weeks, so to say.
In turn, there were possibilities to run away by buying papers, getting permissions to run.

In other words, there were people producing those papers from the Germans, obtaining them from the Germans' authority, from the county clerk at the county offices, and giving out papers to the people to run away.
No, they were regular--
Regular permits to move into to a different section, a different part of Poland.
We obtained those papers for the entire family-- father, mother, and three of us-- and we had to run away.
It was not a question that we could just sit in a car, we had to order a horse and buggy.
And we went with two horses, with a Gentile, and they took us to another town.
30 Kilometers to a town being called Pilica, P-I-L-I-C-A.
We did not have to hide.
We were just-- this town was already, so to say, after the liquidation of the ghetto, so to say, Judenrein-- it was clean of Jews.
So we were just walking around.
We had permission to stay there.
Permission?
Yes, we have permission to stay there.
Absolutely.
That's correct.
We were staying there a few weeks, and then we moved back to Zarki after this town was practically also liquidated.
There were 30 Jews left.
30 Jews were left behind, just the few Jews which were actually the authorities, so to say.
And some were left behind also to clean up the mess that they created.
There were hundreds of people were killed, and quite a disorder in the whole town.

The liquidation of the ghetto, which I stated, was the last day of the holiday of Sukkoth, the Tabernacle, it was that they-- to the actual liquidation of the ghetto, the Germans could not the round up more than 700 people.
The rest of the 4,000 run away like our family, to Pilica and all the other surrounding towns.
The 700 people were loaded into a horse and buggies, brought to the railroad station, put them in a couple of cars, and there were just send them to Treblinka to the gas chambers.
No, my father was with us in the town, and we came back.
Then the-- from the 30 people, they were-- were buying out, by the Germans, stating that they do need more than 30 people to clean up the city.
So in that way they got permission to extend from 30 to 100.
In that 100 group of people, our family was included.
For money.
I was actually not between the 100 counted ones.
The one that was between was my father and my mother, of blessed memory.
I was in the apartment that they lived in, one room, and when they went to work, so to say, I was put between the covers.
Same thing, but they-- one day they-- they were checking those houses for other Jews.
They found my-- being the big man, they caught the big fish.
Yes, I was 14 years old.

They took me to the local jail. 1 was so big, and I was so skinny, and so barely showing life, signs of life-- so that they put me between the ladies, I do remember, the lady-- the lady sections of the jail.
Some how, some way, I had to be rescued during the day, because of the next morning at the daybreak, all the inhabitants of jails were killed.
One of the leaders of the 30 original people came during the day and he bailed me out, seemingly that my father probably paid and he got me out.
Same thing.
That's correct.
Then they're starting again.
The town that people-- they're starting to decimate them again now.
To try to make that they don't need more than 30.
And they tried to send away, again, the 70 people back to-- to the, so to say, where they were gathering in a bigger town.
What the Germans actually do?
Whoever escaped the first time knew that he has to watch it.
But they caught them.
Sooner or later, they caught them again.
They did not kill, because it was a pity to use just a bullet for each Jew.
It's too costly.
So they tried to round up the Jews, again, in various places.
And they put them together, and when they reached the point of 10,000-- that's enough Jews to take a train or train load-- they ordered the trainload of trains and, again, to the gas chamber.
Did they move the population from Zarki to a largest city to a different ghetto?
Yes they did.
They moved them, so they said, here you cannot stay, but you could go back freely and live freely in another ghetto by the name of Radomsko, that's R-A-D-O-M-S-K-O.
It's Nova Radomsko.
Over there they were starting to put the people.
As a matter of fact, even myself was caught one day.
The Germans did not know that my father works there, that I am privileged or not.
I was a Jewish kid.
They put me on a truck and they just brought me into that Radomsko ghetto.
When I arrived in the Radomsko ghetto, again, now being a boy of 14, I did not know where to turn.
I don't know where to go.
And every room that I tried to walk in, they said there's no room.
And I could see there was no room-- that each room was having pritchers of two, three level pritchers--
Bunks.
And there was no room for me.
So I said there is no way.
I have to go back.
I said, go back where?
But I said I'm going to go back.
So the town of Radomsko was 70 kilometers away from-- from my home town.
But I got an idea but-- that the Jews who were rounded up in that truck that I was there with, they were told that their personal possessions-- they were so accurate, they said don't worry about it.
Leave your possessions.
The next truck will come with the possessions after it.
So they brought it.
So it came to my mind when this--the truck came back, brought all those belongings to the Jews, I jumped and I went and I jumped on top of that truck.
And where was that truck heading to?
That truck was heading back to the town that I was born, to Zarki.
It was quite an interesting trip to go back in that truck.
First of all, I jumped at the truck.
He did not know that I am in the truck.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]
Then finally, then-- it was-- that was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I have to take a break.
Excuse me.
The truck was powered by woods, so they had a certain area there where I could go into the to that compartment, and I went into the compartment.
I covered myself.
I covered myself with this, just not to freeze.
So while I was going on-- the truck was going.
I did not even realized where he's heading or where he's going.
Seemingly half-way he stopped the truck.
He stopped the truck, he gets off.
And I didn't even know where, what town, where he is.
Seemingly, I did realize there's a sign, and it said, Przerow.
Small town, which-- I heard of it.
No meaning.
Spelling of it is just for the sake of the testimony.
P-R-Z-E-R-O-W.
He went in-- I looked out.
He went into a bar or a restaurant.
I was definitely not hungry.
And even if I was hungry I could not even ask or dream to say that I am hungry.
So when he came out, seemingly that he wanted to put more fuel into the to that power-- put in woods into the motor, so to say.
I mean the oven.
I don't know how this is being powered, anyhow.
Anyhow, so he took a fork to pick up some woods and to put them into the oven.
So he noticed that this-- he cannot pick up, that somebody's there.
He said, 'who's here?' So he took a flashlight, and he said, 'who's here?' I said, 'I am here.' And I knew the man.
Not that I knew that socially-- I knew who he is.
His name was Haiduck.
I said, what are you doing here?
I said, please, take me to my home town.
My father will definitely pay you that you brought me back.
He knew my father.
Very famous name.
He says, I cannot do that.
I said, but I cannot walk out?
Where should I go?
I cannot go no place.
So he stepped into the-- to the truck, and he drove.
So I said OK, now it seems it's OK.
We were driving close by to the city-- he stops the truck.
And he says to me, you have to get off.
I said, you brought me so far.
I'm on the gates of the city now.
Why do you tell me to get off?
He says look, get off.
I said, I'm not going to get off.
He said, get off.
Back and forth, he's starting to say that I am starting to be very stubborn with him.
He said look, there is curfew in the city that we are driving in.
I have a permit to get back to the town.
The town was on the German border.
The only way that you could do it is just get off the truck, and I will tell you what you should do.
I cannot take you.
They will check the truck, they will find you, you are going to be shot and killed.
I noticed that the man speaks to the point and that he has a point, that I have no way.
So I said, where should I go?
So he said, look, I will tell you what you should do.
And I was seeing that he is quite reasonable.

In other words, he said just go straight down that road, but when you reach the gates of the city, the houses which are practically deserted, the Jewish homes, just get into one of those houses that has the crossing and wait till you see the patrols.
There's a patrol in the city.
Not of the Jews, they're just simply killed because it's curfew.
It's a patrol.
So if you will see the patrol, where they are going in, then you should run to the marketplace.
Otherwise, you won't be able to cross that place.
Sure enough, I was getting into that gate, one of those doors, and I was waiting till the patrol came.
I was at 10, 11 o'clock at night.
Excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So when I noticed the patrol going in from one bar to the other bars-- there were bars for the Germans--
So I-- when they walked in, I crossed the other side.
When I crossed--
To run over-- quite difficult, but I run over.
When I knocked at my father's house where he lived, he was afraid to open the gate of the house.
He was living-- he had a room, one room, in a ground floor level.
So he said look, I will open the window, you will jump through the window, I said, father, I cannot jump.
I hurt myself.
I said I will not be able to jump into the window.
So he put out a chair, and under the chair I stepped, and then we took the chair back into the house.
That how I get back into the hometown.
That still was the part of the hundreds, so to say, 70-- starting to come up again and again-- and they left, again, 30 Jews.
That tried to expel my mother with the children.
Of course, we said we're not going to go to Radomsko.
They want to put us again to Radomsko.

My mother said adamantly, the way she heard what's going on then, and I was there, I witnessed myself, seeing that there's practically no room where to go, where to sleep, that she would not let us go.
Then she found a Gentile man during the day, and she said, would you take my two children-- my sister, myself.
Eli, I don't know where he was.
So he said, yes, gladly, I will take it.
You pay me, I will take the children.
As a matter of fact, he said, I am helping another Jewish family, a Jewish man with two kids.
I would gladly help you, too.
So I said, mother, should I go?
My sister's two years younger.
Should I take sister and go?
She says, you know what, go to your father.
He works probably at this and this place.
And ask your father if he gives you permission to go.
Because I really don't know that man.
Anyhow, I walked over to my father.
I said father, mother tells me to go with this-- with the man, but I don't know who he is, what he is.
Should I go?
He said, my dear child, I cannot answer you the question.
Do as your heart pleases you.
Do it.
So we went.
We went, but my sister was-- the most horrible year.
This was 1942, 1943.
That was the most severe winter.
That was the year that Germany was really defeated at the point of Stalingrad.
And at that point, we were walking in the snow.
My sister really was not able to walk in the high snow going in and out and out.
Finally, at the late evening hour, we reached the house.
And we went and he put us in into the cellar.
My sister was hungry.
She's younger.
She said, I'm hungry.
I want to eat something.
I'm hungry.
And she's starting to cry.
I said, I have nothing what to give.
So I was seeing there is a Jew with two children there on the corner.
I said to him, maybe you have something for my sister?
I am not hungry.
My sister is hungry.
So he said, I don't have my own two children any food to give you, or to spare.
But I can give you one advice.
On the other corner of the cellar, there is a barrel.
Be careful.
On the top of the barrel there's a sauerkraut-- it's cabbage there.
And there's a big stone.
If you could push a little bit-- don't push the stone-- if you could push your small and skinny arm, maybe you could get out.
So there was a big barrel there with cabbage, and I put my little arm in there and I managed to get out a little bit of--
Strands of cabbage.
And it was sour.
So I gave it to my sister and she says, oh, that's so good.
It's so delicious, so good.
So we stayed there quite a few hours, and seemingly, that my mother's supposed to come to.
And he went back instead.
The same man.
So the same man, expecting that mother's coming back, he brought back a letter from my father and he said, my dear children, I want you back.
I want you dearly back.
Go and listen to that man.
He's going to take you back to town.
I knew that we won't be able to walk.
He said, no, no.
Your father paid enough money.
I hired a sled with horses.
It was high snow, very big snow.
We had a sled with horses and he took us back into the town.
I said, but where will you take us?
I don't know where my father-- He said, no, your father said-- at daybreak he will be waiting at the cemetery.
So my father, there, standing in subfreezing weather, waiting-- not knowing exactly the time and hour when the two poor children would come.
And he was waiting, and he embraced us and he was crying and he took us back.
And he gave us a place, again now, to go in a house where mother was there.
Now what arrangements were made afterwards to try to save yourself?

So then this was already going in towards the end of 1942, going into beginning of 1943 where we found out that this Radomsko ghetto, that this famous ghetto, was completely-- 10,000 Jews were sent off to Treblinka, to the gas chamber.
At that time was no way already to go anyplace.
This was it.

And my father, luckily, secured through negotiation-- probably for a long time with a good friend of my father, which he knew and had befriended himself many years before-- and he built a bunker in the town.
Yes, he was quite a hand.
First of all, he was an architect from profession, himself, and he was an intelligent man.
And he did it.
He risked his life and his family.
I want, at this point, just to bring out one more point-- the hardship about the Poles upon the Jews.
It was not a question of collaboration, of helping.
The only country in Europe, where that sentence for helping or hiding a Jew, was Poland, and Poland only.
The Jew in Holland or in France for helping a Jew were not severely punished the way they were punished in Poland.
Exactly.
The name of the person who built this bunker for us was Yusef and Maria Placzek, P-L-A-C-Z-E-K.
He built two bunkers-- as a matter of fact.
The one, just to be able to sustain.
Two, another one in case of, really, danger.
I would describe the first one where most of the time we spent.
It was on the attic whereby the-- on the attic, we could sleep there.
It was cold, but we could eat and live.
The house was an unfinished house, which was-- in a part, was good.
Because they had no stairs, so we had a ladder.
